hazard.summary: Two parallel spawned child task agents hit the same account-level rate limit simultaneously. Each receives HTTP 429 with retry-after-ms ~11180000 (~3 hours). The STOP condition is not propagated to the parent session, which continues to appear in-progress for over an hour until manually cancelled.
signals: HTTP 429 with body: {"type":"error","error":{"type":"rate_limit_error","message":"This request would exceed your account's rate limit. Please try again later."}}
retry-after-ms=11180000 (~3 hours) on child task A
retry-after-ms=11179000 (~3 hours) on child task B
Two parallel child tasks hitting same account-level limit simultaneously
Parent session stuck in review phase for over an hour
Recovery only possible via manual cancellation
stopReason=error in both child session JSONL logs

constraints: retry-after values exceeding a configurable threshold must be classified as STOP, not retryable pressure.
STOP conditions in child tasks must be propagated to the parent session immediately.
Parent session must not appear in-progress when all child tasks are blocked on STOP conditions.
Concurrent spawned agents sharing the same account limits must be treated as a shared quota pool, not independent retry units.
retry-after-ms must be surfaced to the parent so it knows when recovery is possible.
knobs: parent session state when child tasks are blocked
fail-fast or timeout behavior for long retry windows
verification: Spawn two parallel child task agents against a rate-limited account.
Verify both children surface STOP immediately on retry-after > threshold.
Verify parent session reflects blocked state within seconds of child STOP.
Verify parent does not remain in-progress when all children are blocked.
Verify retry-after value is visible in parent session state.
Verify no retry loop is entered for retry-after values exceeding threshold.
notes: First receipt documenting STOP propagation failure in a parent/child multi-agent session. Previous receipts document STOP misclassification within a single retry loop. This case shows STOP classified correctly at the child level but not propagated upward — the parent has no visibility into child blocked state. Confirmed via real JSONL session logs from two parallel child agents. Both hit the same Anthropic account-level rate limit within 1 second of each other (timestamps 1775148765808 and 1775148765804), consistent with concurrent spawned agents sharing a single account quota pool. Subsequent maintainer testing suggested the visible false-liveness symptom may also interact with token reauthentication or other shared session state under multiple parallel sessions, but that mechanism was not yet confirmed at capture time.

constraints: child STOP must propagate to parent immediately
parent must not appear in-progress when children are blocked
anti_patterns: parent session remaining in-progress while children are blocked
STOP condition silently absorbed at child level
concurrent agents treated as independent quota consumers
